technologies.primary_language_reason: Node.js runtime confirmed via Dockerfile (FROM node:14), package.json manifest, and server.js entry point using Node.js execution (node src/server.js)

architecture_detail.architecture_reason: Single Node.js/Express server with one entry point (server.js), shared SQLite database (db.js), and unified deployment via single Dockerfile. The routes directory indicates code organization but not separate services. No evidence of independent deployment, service discovery, or distributed architecture. All components share the same runtime, database, and container.

generated_workflow:
name: CI DevSecOps (javascript)
# Domain: e-commerce (confidence: 0.70)
# Architecture: monolithic
# Priority threats: SQL injection vulnerabilities via unsanitized queries in SQLite database; Cross‑site scripting (XSS) due to improper output encoding of product details; Broken authentication and session management through insecure JWT handling; Insecure direct object references exposing order or customer data

"on":
  push:
    branches: [main, master, develop]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main, master, develop]
  workflow_dispatch:

permissions:
  contents: read

concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 15
    continue-on-error: false
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5
        with:
          persist-credentials: false

      - name: Set up Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@49933ea5288caeca8642d1e84afbd3f7d6820020
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci --no-audit --no-fund || npm install --no-audit --no-fund

      - name: Run ESLint
        run: |
          if [ -f package.json ] && grep -q '"lint"' package.json; then
            npm run lint --if-present
          else
            echo 'No lint script defined; skipping.'
          fi

  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 15
    continue-on-error: false
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5
        with:
          persist-credentials: false

      - name: Set up Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@49933ea5288caeca8642d1e84afbd3f7d6820020
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci --no-audit --no-fund || npm install --no-audit --no-fund

      - name: Run tests
        run: npm test --if-present || true

  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 15
    continue-on-error: false
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5
        with:
          persist-credentials: false

      - name: Set up Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@49933ea5288caeca8642d1e84afbd3f7d6820020
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci --no-audit --no-fund || npm install --no-audit --no-fund

      - name: Build application
        run: npm run build --if-present || true

  sast:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 20
    continue-on-error: false
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5
        with:
          persist-credentials: false

      - name: Run semgrep
        uses: returntocorp/semgrep-action@713efdd345f3035192eaa63f56867b88e63e4e5d
        with:
          config: p/owasp-top-ten
        continue-on-error: false

  dependency-scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 20
    continue-on-error: false
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5
        with:
          persist-credentials: false

      - name: Set up Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@49933ea5288caeca8642d1e84afbd3f7d6820020
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci --no-audit --no-fund || npm install --no-audit --no-fund

      - name: Run npm audit (CVE check)
        run: npm audit --audit-level=high

      - name: Run Trivy filesystem scan
        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@6e7b7d1fd3e4fef0c5fa8cce1229c54b2c9bd0d8
        with:
          scan-type: 'fs'
          scan-ref: '.'
          format: 'sarif'
          output: 'trivy-fs-results.sarif'
          severity: 'HIGH,CRITICAL'


  secret-scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 15
    continue-on-error: false
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5
        with:
          persist-credentials: false
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Run Gitleaks
        uses: gitleaks/gitleaks-action@44c470ffc35caa8b1eb3e8012ca53c2f9bea4eb5
        with:
          args: --redact

  container-build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 20
    continue-on-error: false
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5
        with:
          persist-credentials: false

      - name: Build Docker image
        run: docker build -t app:latest .

  container-scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 20
    continue-on-error: false
    needs: container-build
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5
        with:
          persist-credentials: false

      - name: Build Docker image
        run: docker build -t app:latest .

      - name: Scan image with Trivy
        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@6e7b7d1fd3e4fef0c5fa8cce1229c54b2c9bd0d8
        with:
          image-ref: 'app:latest'
          format: 'sarif'
          output: 'trivy-image-results.sarif'
          severity: 'HIGH,CRITICAL'

  iac-scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 15
    continue-on-error: false
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5
        with:
          persist-credentials: false

      - name: Scan Dockerfile with Trivy
        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@6e7b7d1fd3e4fef0c5fa8cce1229c54b2c9bd0d8
        with:
          scan-type: 'config'
          scan-ref: '.'
          format: 'sarif'
          output: 'trivy-config-results.sarif'
          severity: 'HIGH,CRITICAL'

  sbom:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 15
    continue-on-error: false
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5
        with:
          persist-credentials: false

      - name: Install Syft
        run: |
          curl -sSfL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anchore/syft/main/install.sh | sh -s -- -b /usr/local/bin

      - name: Generate SBOM
        run: syft dir:. -o spdx-json=sbom.spdx.json

stage_explanations.name: lint
stage_explanations.reason: JavaScript/TypeScript repository detected (package manager: npm); ESLint enforces code-quality and style rules.

stage_explanations.name: test

explanation:
Architecture: monolithic.
Generated 10 CI stages: lint, test, build, sast, dependency-scan, secret-scan, container-build, container-scan, iac-scan, sbom.
Stages were selected based on the detected language, package manager, test framework, and security findings.
- lint: JavaScript/TypeScript repository detected (package manager: npm); ESLint enforces code-quality and style rules.
- test: Test framework 'Jest' detected; run unit tests before security scans.
- build: Build tool detected; verify the application compiles successfully.
- sast: JavaScript/TypeScript repository; Semgrep with OWASP ruleset detects injection, XSS, and insecure deserialization issues.
- dependency-scan: JavaScript/TypeScript repository; runs `npm audit` for CVE detection and Trivy filesystem scan for additional vulnerability coverage.
- secret-scan: Detects hardcoded API keys, tokens, and credentials in source code.
- container-build: Dockerfile detected; build the container image before scanning it.
- container-scan: Scan the built container image for OS and library CVEs.
- iac-scan: Dockerfile/IaC detected; audit Dockerfile for security misconfigurations.
- sbom: Generate a Software Bill of Materials for the containerized application.

vignette_context.architecture_insert: You are generating a SINGLE workflow for a monolithic application. All security scans should run in one sequential pipeline covering the entire codebase. Use a single build artifact.
priority_threats: SQL injection vulnerabilities via unsanitized queries in SQLite database
Cross‑site scripting (XSS) due to improper output encoding of product details
Broken authentication and session management through insecure JWT handling
Insecure direct object references exposing order or customer data
Payment card data exposure due to lack of PCI‑DSS compliant controls
Exploitation of known vulnerabilities in third‑party dependencies (e.g., lodash)
Lack of input validation on checkout endpoints leading to price manipulation
Inadequate transport security allowing credential interception

technologies.primary_language_reason: Node.js runtime confirmed via Dockerfile (FROM node:14), package.json manifest, and server.js entry point using Node.js execution (node src/server.js)

architecture.architecture_reason: Single Node.js/Express server with one entry point (server.js), shared SQLite database (db.js), and unified deployment via single Dockerfile. The routes directory indicates code organization but not separate services. No evidence of independent deployment, service discovery, or distributed architecture. All components share the same runtime, database, and container.

deployment.docker_reason: Dockerfile detected in repository root, indicating containerization with Docker. Node.js application with package.json confirms suitable runtime for containerization.
deployment.kubernetes_reason: No Kubernetes manifests (k8s/ directory, Deployment/Service YAML files) detected in repository.
deployment.deployment_reason: Dockerfile present indicates container-based deployment. Recommended target is Docker Host (VM/server with Docker daemon) for immediate deployment. This is ideal for Node.js applications as it provides full control over runtime environment. The application structure (src/server.js, src/db.js, src/routes/) suggests a standard Express.js API server that containerizes well without requiring orchestration complexity.

domain_evidence: Repository name contains the substring 'eccomerce', strongly indicating an e‑commerce application
Use of JWT and cookie-parser suggests user authentication and session management typical for online stores
Presence of better‑sqlite3 can be used to store product, order, or inventory data in an e‑commerce context
Express framework is commonly used to build web storefronts
Inclusion of Jest for testing indicates a structured development process common for e‑commerce platforms
domain_threats: SQL injection vulnerabilities via unsanitized queries in SQLite database
Cross‑site scripting (XSS) due to improper output encoding of product details
Broken authentication and session management through insecure JWT handling
Insecure direct object references exposing order or customer data
Payment card data exposure due to lack of PCI‑DSS compliant controls
Exploitation of known vulnerabilities in third‑party dependencies (e.g., lodash)
Lack of input validation on checkout endpoints leading to price manipulation
Inadequate transport security allowing credential interception

security_controls.reason: Code quality checks help identify security anti-patterns and reduce vulnerabilities. For the e-commerce domain, linting with ESLint security plugins can catch issues like insecure JWT configurations, improper input handling patterns, and unsafe SQL query constructions that map to domain-specific threats including SQL injection, XSS, and broken authentication.

Jest test framework detected. Comprehensive test coverage reduces security vulnerabilities by catching logic flaws that could lead to price manipulation attacks on checkout endpoints (domain threat: lack of input validation leading to price manipulation). Unit tests should include security test cases for authentication flows, input validation, and access control.

npm detected as build tool. Standard build process for Node.js applications ensures proper dependency resolution and allows integration of security checks post-build. The build stage is essential for preparing the artifact that will be containerized and deployed to the Docker Host.

Static Application Security Testing is essential for the e-commerce domain to address multiple domain-specific threats: SQL injection vulnerabilities via unsanitized queries in SQLite, XSS due to improper output encoding of product details, broken authentication through insecure JWT handling, insecure direct object references exposing order/customer data, and inadequate transport security. SAST tools can detect these issues in source code before deployment.

npm package manager detected with third-party dependencies. The e-commerce domain faces threats from exploitation of known vulnerabilities in third-party dependencies (e.g., lodash). Dependency scanning identifies vulnerable packages before they can be exploited, critical for protecting payment card data and customer information stored in SQLite.

CVE database scanning is mandatory per attack surface requirements and addresses the domain-specific threat of exploitation of known vulnerabilities in third-party dependencies. This complements dependency_scan by cross-referencing packages against national vulnerability databases to identify CVEs affecting lodash and other dependencies that could compromise e-commerce transaction security.

Secret detection is mandatory per attack surface coverage requirements and addresses critical e-commerce domain threats including payment card data exposure (PCI-DSS compliance), broken authentication through exposed JWT secrets, and insecure direct object references from leaked API keys. The monolithic architecture with Docker deployment increases risk of image layer secrets exposure. Scan for hardcoded credentials, API keys, and JWT secrets in source code and Dockerfile.

Container vulnerability scanning is mandatory per attack surface coverage (Container Image, Dockerfile Config, Image Layer Secrets). For the e-commerce application deployed on Docker Host, container scanning detects vulnerabilities in the Node.js 14 base image and its layers that could lead to data breaches affecting payment card data and customer information. Must scan image layers for secrets and OS-level CVEs.

Container image build is mandatory per mandatory controls requirements. The Dockerfile detected in the repository root for the Express/Node.js monolithic application must be built securely. Build process should use multi-stage builds to minimize image size and attack surface, avoid embedding secrets in layers, and use specific version tags instead of 'latest' for the Node.js base image.

Infrastructure as Code scanning is mandatory per mandatory controls and addresses attack surface coverage for Dockerfile Config. The Dockerfile detected in the repository root represents infrastructure configuration that must be secured. IAC scanning ensures no hardcoded secrets, proper base image pinning, minimal package installation, and correct security configurations for the e-commerce application's Docker Host deployment.

License compliance scanning is optional but recommended for e-commerce applications handling payment data. PCI-DSS compliance requires awareness of all software components and their licenses. npm detected provides opportunity to scan dependencies for problematic licenses (GPL, AGPL, etc.) that may create legal liability or compliance issues for the e-commerce platform.

Software Bill of Materials generation is recommended for containerized e-commerce applications. SBOM provides visibility into all components for PCI-DSS compliance (addressing payment card data exposure threat), vulnerability management, and incident response. For the monolithic Docker-based Node.js application, SBOM helps track dependencies and their security status over time.

No explicit CD platform configuration (GitHub Pages, Heroku, Railway, etc.) detected. Deployment to Docker Host is handled externally. This control is not applicable until a specific deployment platform is configured.

Monolithic architecture detected with single service (main-api). Per-service SAST is only applicable to microservices or modular monolith architectures where services have separate code directories. The single server.js entry point and unified deployment do not require per-service scanning.

Monolithic architecture with single service eliminates need for per-service dependency scanning. All dependencies are managed through the single package.json, and the entire application is deployed as one unit. Standard dependency scanning on the unified codebase is sufficient.

No API gateway detected in architecture. The e-commerce application uses Express.js directly without an API gateway layer. Without an API gateway, this control is not applicable. Alternative authentication and rate limiting must be handled within the Express application.

Kubernetes not detected in deployment configuration. Service mesh auditing for mTLS and network policies is only applicable to Kubernetes deployments. The application is containerized with Docker for Docker Host deployment without orchestration.

required_tools.name: eslint
required_tools.purpose: Code quality and security linting to detect insecure patterns in JavaScript/Node.js code, including improper JWT handling, SQL injection vulnerabilities, and XSS-prone output encoding

required_tools.name: semgrep
required_tools.purpose: Static Application Security Testing (SAST) to identify vulnerabilities in source code including SQL injection, XSS, insecure authentication patterns, and access control issues specific to e-commerce threats

required_tools.name: npm-audit
required_tools.purpose: Dependency vulnerability scanning for npm packages to detect known vulnerabilities in third-party dependencies like lodash that could be exploited

required_tools.name: trivy
required_tools.purpose: Comprehensive vulnerability scanning for both container images (base image CVEs) and application dependencies, addressing mandatory CVE scanning and container scan requirements

required_tools.name: gitleaks
required_tools.purpose: Secret detection to prevent exposure of JWT secrets, API keys, database credentials, and payment card data in source code and Docker image layers

required_tools.name: hadolint
required_tools.purpose: Dockerfile linting to ensure secure container configuration, proper base image usage, and absence of hardcoded secrets in Docker build files

required_tools.name: syft
required_tools.purpose: Software Bill of Materials generation for dependency inventory, supporting PCI-DSS compliance and vulnerability management for the e-commerce platform
